bones.name: Clavicle
description: The bony structure of the thoracic wall includes the thoracic vertebrae, the sternum, and the ribs.
) articulate with the sternum by individual costal cartilages.
The cartilages of ribs 8-10 articulate with the cartilages of the rib above.
because they do not attach to the sternum at all.
consists of the clavicle and scapula and connects the upper limb to the trunk.

bones.name: No labels
description: Also known as the breast bone.
Consists of a superior manubrium, intermediate body, and inferior xiphoid process.
It is convex on its anterior surface and concave on its posterior surface.
It articulates with the clavicles superiorly and the cartilages of the upper 7 ribs inferiorly.
The sternal fibers of pectoralis major  muscle originate from the anterior surface of the manubrium and body.
The sternothyroid and sternohyoid muscles originate from the posterior surface of the manubrium.
Transversus thoracis muscle attaches to the posterior surface of the body and xiphoid process.
The medial fibers of rectus abdominus attach to the anterior surface of the xiphoid process.
The linea alba attaches to the inferior border of the xiphoid process.

bones.name: Manubrium
description: is a shallow depression in the superior border and is found at the level between the 2
indicate where the clavicles articulate with the manubrium at the sternoclavicular joints.
ribs attach to the lateral borders.
marks the junction of the manubrium and body
It is found at the level between the 4
It attaches to the second ribs
It is an important surface landmark.

bones.name: Body
description: It articulates with the costal cartilages of ribs 2-7 on is lateral borders.
It articulates with the manubrium superiorly and the xiphoid process inferiorly.

bones.name: Xiphoid process
description: Articulates with the body superiorly and the inferior part of the 7
costal cartilage on its upper lateral border.
It is slightly recessed and may be perforated.
It remains cartilaginous longer than the rest of the sternum.

bones.name: Clavicle
description: Also known as the collar bone.
It is the most frequently broken bone in the body.
It is convex medially and concave laterally on its anterior surface.
It articulates medially with the manubrium of the sternum and laterally with the acromion process of the scapula.
Acts as a brace or strut for the shoulder and allows the upper limb to move freely on the trunk.

bones.name: Shaft
description: Provides proximal attachment for the pectoralis major muscle on its anterior surface.
The subclavius muscle originates on and lies in a groove on the posterior surface.
provides attachment for the trapezoid part of the coracoclavicular ligament*.
provides attachment for the conoid part of the coracoclavicular ligament*.
connects the clavicle to the upper surface of the first rib and its cartilage.
* The coracoclavicular ligament connects the clavicle to the coracoid process of the scapula.

bones.name: Acromial end
description: The lateral end that articulates with the acromion process of the scapula.
Provides distal attachment for the trapezius muscle on the superior and posterior surface.
Provides proximal attachment for the deltoid muscle on the superior and anterior surface.

bones.name: Sternal end
description: The medial end that articulates with the manubrium of the sternum.
Provides attachment for the lateral fibers of the sternohyoid muscle on the posterior surface.
Provides proximal attachment for the clavicular head of the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

bones.name: First Rib
description: The first rib is the shortest, broadest and most curved.
It attaches to the manubrium of the sternum just inferior to the sternoclavicular joint.

bones.name: Head and Neck
description: is small and round and articulates with the body of the first thoracic vertebra.
is a constricted portion situated between the head and the tubercle.

bones.name: Tubercles
description: articulates with the transverse process of the first thoracic vertebra.
is a small pointed projection on the superior surface.
It provides distal attachment for the anterior scalene muscle.

bones.name: Grooves for subclavian vessels
description: lies in a shallow groove just anterior to the scalene tubercle.
lies in a shallow groove just posterior to the scalene tubercle.
The lower trunk of the brachial plexus also passes posterior to the scalene tubercle.

bones.name: Typical rib
description: They are longer and narrower than the 1
Each rib contains a head that articulates with at least one thoracic vertebrae.

bones.name: Head and Neck
description: of each rib will articulate with the bodies of 2 vertebrae and an intervertebral disc.
It contains a pair of articular facets (superior and inferior).
The superior facet will articulate with the body of the superior vertebra.
The inferior facet will articulate with the body of the inferior vertebra.
The heads of ribs 10-12 have a single facet and articulate with their own vertebrae.
is a constricted portion just lateral to the head.
pair of ribs have no necks.

bones.name: Tubercle
description: The tubercle of each typical rib is a knob like structure on the posterior surface where the neck joins the body.
It articulates with the transverse process of the corresponding vertebra.

bones.name: Body
description: The body (shaft) is the main part of the rib.
The angle is an abrupt change in curvature that occurs laterally to the tubercle.
The costal groove is a shallow depression on the internal surface of the inferior border of the body.
It contains the neurovascular bundle for each intercostal space*.
*This is important to know when performing a chest tube procedure.

bones.name: Scapula
description: The scapula is sometimes referred to as the “shoulder blade.”
It is a large, triangular, flat bone situated in the superior part of the posterior thoracic wall.
It lies between the levels of the second and seventh ribs.

bones.name: Labels
description: The body of the scapula contains 3 borders.
is lateral and provides proximal attachment for the teres minor and part of the teres major muscles.
is medial and provides distal attachment for the rhomboideus major (below the spine), rhomboideus minor (at the spine), and levator scapulae (above the spine), and serratus anterior muscles.
provides  proximal attachment for the inferior belly of the omohyoid muscle.
The superior border also contains the
The notch is bridged by the superior transverse scapular ligament.
The ligament and notch create a foramen that transmits the suprascapular nerve.
provides proximal attachment for part of the teres major and latissumus dorsi muscles.
is between the superior and vertebral borders.

bones.name: Spine
description: The spine of the scapula is a prominent ridge on the posterior surface and is triangular in shape.
It separates the posterior surface into 2 fossae.
The supraspinous fossa above and the infraspinous fossa below.
It broadens laterally as the acromion process.
It provides proximal attachment for part of the deltoid muscle.
The uppermost fibers of the trapezius muscle partially insert on the spine.

bones.name: Labels
description: The anterior (costal) surface is known as the
and provides attachment for the subscapularis muscle.
The posterior (dorsal) surface is divided into the supraspinous and infraspinous fossa by the spine of the scapula.
provides the origin of the supraspinatus muscle.
provides the origin of the infraspinatus muscle.
The lateral angle presents the glenoid fossa.
is a shallow depression that articulates with the head of the humerus to create a ball-and-socket type synovial joint.
sits above the glenoid fossa and provides the origin for the long head of the biceps brachii muscle.
sits below the glenoid fossa and provides the origin for the long head of the triceps brachii muscle.

bones.name: Acromion and Coracoid process
description: is a broad projection from the lateral end of the spine.
It curves anteriorly to articulate with the clavicle to form the acromioclavicular joint.
It provides part of the origin for the deltoid muscle and part of the insertion for the trapezius muscle.
is a hook-like projection from the superior border between the scapular notch and the glenoid fossa.
The coracobrachialis muscle and short head of the biceps brachii muscle arise from the coracoid process
The pectoralis minor inserts on the coracoid process.
